title: The Math Says Aliens Should Be Everywhere. So Where Are They?
hook: There are an estimated hundreds of billions of planets in our galaxy alone, and the universe is old enough for intelligent life to have had billions of years' head start on us.
body: That contradiction has a name, the Fermi Paradox, after the physicist who reportedly blurted out 'where is everybody' during a lunch conversation in 1950. The proposed answers range from comforting to unsettling. Maybe intelligent life is genuinely rare because the specific chain of events on Earth was a fluke. Maybe civilizations tend to destroy themselves or lose interest in expansion before they can spread across the galaxy. Or maybe interstellar distances are simply a harder wall than science fiction makes them look, and everyone is equally stuck close to home. No version of the answer has enough evidence yet to close the debate.
cta: Which answer would you actually want to be true? Follow for more of the universe's open questions.
description: The Fermi Paradox in under a minute: if the odds favor intelligent life being common, why haven't we found any sign of it?
